ut1-1-1.title: Hands-on: Your first pulse-echo measurement
ut1-1-1.intro: Firestone's 1940 pulse-echo idea, live: slide a 0° probe across a stepped steel block and watch the backwall echo arrive later wherever the metal is thicker. Measure three steps and pick out the thinnest — the same single-sided thickness survey run on pipes and vessels every day.

tasks.label: Record thickness readings on three different steps
tasks.hint: Park the probe fully on a step; the backwall echo's round-trip time × velocity ÷ 2 is the thickness.

tasks.label: Identify the thinnest step of the block
tasks.hint: Thinner metal means a shorter round trip — its backwall echo arrives earliest on the A-scan.

ut1-1-2.title: Hands-on: Make wavelength obey λ = v ÷ f
ut1-1-2.intro: Put the master equation to work: sweep the probe frequency and swap between steel, aluminium, and water while the wavefront spacing tracks λ = v ÷ f live. Confirm the lesson's shortcut — λ in mm equals velocity in mm/µs divided by frequency in MHz.

tasks.label: Get the wavelength below 1 mm in steel
tasks.hint: In steel λ = 5.92 ÷ f (mm, with f in MHz) — push the frequency past 5.92 MHz.

tasks.label: Send the pulse through steel, aluminium, and water
tasks.hint: Frequency belongs to the probe and stays put; velocity belongs to the material, so λ jumps at each swap.
